content.text: A voice pipeline is the plumbing that turns a person's spoken words into a response they can hear back — in under 1.5 seconds. It chains three AI models (STT, LLM, TTS) and several infrastructure pieces into a continuous loop that must be fast, fault-tolerant, and conversationally coherent. Every design choice in this pipeline involves a latency trade-off, and understanding those trade-offs is the difference between a voice agent that feels alive and one that feels laggy and broken.
is an end-to-end system that converts a user's speech into a spoken AI response. It links four processing stages in sequence:
— decides when the user has started and stopped speaking, so the downstream stages process complete utterances rather than continuous noise.
— converts the audio waveform into a text transcript that the LLM can process.
— generates the response text, optionally invoking tools before replying.
— synthesizes the LLM's text into audio that plays through the user's speaker.

Each stage has its own latency budget. The sum of all four stages — plus network round-trips and buffering — determines whether the agent feels responsive. The industry target for natural-feeling conversation is a total round-trip below 1.5 seconds from end-of-speech to start-of-response audio.

Voice is the highest-bandwidth communication channel humans use. Text-based chat agents require users to type, read, and context-switch. Voice agents remove all of that friction: a user can ask a question while driving, cooking, or working. For customer service, medical triage, accessibility tools, and real-time translation, voice is not a nice-to-have — it is the only viable interface.

But voice is also brutally intolerant of latency. In a text chat, a 3-second response is acceptable. In a spoken conversation, 3 seconds feels like the other person has stopped listening. A voice agent with a 2-second round-trip will be abandoned; one with a 800ms round-trip will feel magical. The pipeline design — every queuing decision, every streaming choice — is in service of shrinking that number.
— speaking is faster and less cognitively demanding than typing.
— accessible when eyes or hands are occupied.
— each stage (STT, LLM, TTS) can be swapped independently as better models ship.
— LLM token streaming means TTS can begin synthesizing before the full response is generated, slashing perceived latency.
— choosing STT and TTS models that cover the same language is straightforward; the LLM layer handles translation if needed.
— an STT transcription error silently corrupts the LLM's input. The LLM responds to a wrong question and the user hears a confident wrong answer.
— each stage adds latency. A worst-case STT (600ms) + LLM TTFT (800ms) + TTS (400ms) path exceeds the 1.5s target before network is even counted.
— VAD tuned for silence suppression may cut off soft-spoken users; tuned loosely, it forwards noise as speech.
— interrupting the agent mid-speech requires cancelling an in-progress TTS stream, discarding already-queued audio, and resetting conversation state — all within milliseconds.
— running three model APIs (STT, LLM, TTS) simultaneously is more expensive and has more failure modes than a single text-chat API.
— models like GPT-4o Audio and Gemini Live bypass STT and TTS entirely by consuming and emitting raw audio tokens. They are currently less controllable but shrink the pipeline to one API call.

The pipeline runs as a stateful loop. Audio frames (typically 20ms chunks of PCM at 16kHz) stream continuously from the client's microphone over a WebSocket connection. The VAD model scores each frame for voice probability. When the probability crosses a threshold, the VAD opens a speech segment. When it falls below the threshold for a configurable silence period (typically 500–800ms), the segment is considered complete and the accumulated audio is forwarded to STT.

The STT model transcribes the segment and returns a text string. That text is appended to the conversation history (which is maintained server-side) and the full history is sent to the LLM. Crucially, the LLM is configured to stream its response — rather than waiting for the complete reply, the pipeline receives tokens as they are generated. A sentence splitter watches the token stream for natural sentence boundaries (periods, question marks, em dashes). When a sentence boundary is found, that sentence is immediately dispatched to TTS.

TTS synthesis also streams — it returns audio chunks before the entire sentence is synthesized. Those chunks are buffered on the client and played sequentially. This double-streaming (LLM tokens → sentence → TTS audio) means the user hears the first word of the response while the LLM is still generating sentence two and TTS has not yet started on sentence three. The result is a perceived latency that closely matches the LLM's Time-To-First-Token (TTFT) rather than the total generation time.
A state machine governs the conversation turn lifecycle:
. The pipeline can only accept new speech in
state, preventing it from confusing the agent's own TTS output as new user input (acoustic echo). Barge-in support adds a transition from
when VAD detects strong user speech, cancelling the current TTS playback.

attrs.alt: Voice pipeline architecture diagram showing VAD → STT → LLM → TTS stages with timing annotations and WebSocket transport layer
attrs.title: Voice Pipeline Architecture

content.text: architecture where each model is a separate API call connected by a WebSocket transport layer. The latency bar at the bottom shows the typical contribution of each stage to the total round-trip time. Note the tool-calls branch off the LLM — when the model decides to invoke an external function (e.g., a calendar lookup), that side-trip adds 50–500ms before the LLM can begin streaming its verbal response.
is a requirement and typing is difficult for the target audience.
that replace or augment IVR systems.
— multi-turn, open-ended questions — rather than command-and-control (where wake-word systems suffice).
over the pipeline — custom STT for domain vocabulary, specific TTS voice, or specialized LLM system prompt — as opposed to end-to-end models which are harder to customize.

Prefer an end-to-end model (GPT-4o Realtime, Gemini Live) over a cascaded pipeline when: round-trip latency below 500ms is required, you don't need transcript access, and the conversational domain is general enough that a pre-trained voice personality is acceptable.
A production voice pipeline requires the following infrastructure pieces:
— the central hub that accepts audio from the client and orchestrates calls to STT, LLM, and TTS services.
— Silero VAD (ONNX model, ~1MB) is a common choice; it runs on CPU and classifies 30ms frames in under 1ms.
— Deepgram Nova-2 or AssemblyAI for low-latency streaming transcription; Whisper (self-hosted) for privacy-sensitive deployments.
— any OpenAI-compatible endpoint with Server-Sent Events streaming. System prompt must include a persona and instructions to keep responses concise.
— ElevenLabs, OpenAI TTS, or Cartesia for low-latency streaming synthesis.
— in-memory dict keyed by session ID; flush to Redis for multi-server deployments.
never wait for the previous stage to complete before starting the next

. Start STT transcription as soon as the audio segment is closed. Start sending text to TTS as soon as the first sentence boundary is detected in the LLM stream. Start playing audio as soon as the first TTS chunk arrives.
Full voice pipeline server with FastAPI and WebSockets

# voice_pipeline.py — minimal but production-patterned voice pipeline
import asyncio
import json
import time
from enum import Enum
from typing import AsyncIterator

import numpy as np
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket, WebSocketDisconnect
from openai import AsyncOpenAI

# pip install fastapi uvicorn openai deepgram-sdk elevenlabs numpy

app = FastAPI()
openai_client = AsyncOpenAI()  # reads OPENAI_API_KEY from env


class TurnState(Enum):
    IDLE = "idle"
    LISTENING = "listening"
    THINKING = "thinking"
    SPEAKING = "speaking"


class VoiceSession:
    def __init__(self, session_id: str):
        self.session_id = session_id
        self.state = TurnState.IDLE
        self.history: list[dict] = []
        self.audio_buffer: list[bytes] = []
        self.cancel_tts = asyncio.Event()

    def append_audio(self, chunk: bytes) -> None:
        self.audio_buffer.append(chunk)

    def flush_audio(self) -> bytes:
        audio = b"".join(self.audio_buffer)
        self.audio_buffer.clear()
        return audio

    def transition(self, new_state: TurnState) -> None:
        print(f"[{self.session_id}] {self.state.value} → {new_state.value}")
        self.state = new_state


async def vad_is_speech(frame: bytes, threshold: float = 0.5) -> bool:
    """Stub: replace with Silero VAD ONNX inference.
    In production, call model.infer(frame) and compare to threshold."""
    rms = np.sqrt(np.mean(np.frombuffer(frame, dtype=np.int16).astype(float) ** 2))
    return rms > 800  # crude energy-based placeholder


async def transcribe(audio_bytes: bytes) -> str:
    """STT via Deepgram Nova-2 (streaming would be preferred; this shows the shape)."""
    from deepgram import DeepgramClient, PrerecordedOptions

    dg = DeepgramClient()  # reads DEEPGRAM_API_KEY from env
    options = PrerecordedOptions(model="nova-2", language="en-US", smart_format=True)
    response = await asyncio.to_thread(
        dg.listen.prerecorded.v("1").transcribe_file,
        {"buffer": audio_bytes, "mimetype": "audio/raw;rate=16000"},
        options,
    )
    return response.results.channels[0].alternatives[0].transcript


async def stream_llm(history: list[dict]) -> AsyncIterator[str]:
    """Stream tokens from the LLM. Yields complete sentence chunks."""
    sentence_buf = ""
    sentence_endings = {".", "!", "?", "\n"}

    stream = await openai_client.chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[
            {"role": "system", "content": (
                "You are a helpful voice assistant. Keep each response concise and "
                "conversational — two sentences maximum unless asked to elaborate. "
                "Do not use markdown, bullet points, or headers in your responses."
            )},
            *history,
        ],
        stream=True,
        max_tokens=200,
    )

    async for chunk in stream:
        delta = chunk.choices[0].delta.content or ""
        sentence_buf += delta
        # Yield a sentence as soon as we see an ending punctuation
        for i, ch in enumerate(sentence_buf):
            if ch in sentence_endings and i > 0:
                yield sentence_buf[: i + 1].strip()
                sentence_buf = sentence_buf[i + 1 :]
                break

    if sentence_buf.strip():
        yield sentence_buf.strip()


async def synthesize_sentence(sentence: str) -> bytes:
    """TTS via OpenAI TTS API. Returns raw MP3 bytes."""
    response = await openai_client.audio.speech.create(
        model="tts-1",
        voice="alloy",
        input=sentence,
        response_format="mp3",
    )
    return response.content


@app.websocket("/voice/{session_id}")
async def voice_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket, session_id: str):
    await websocket.accept()
    session = VoiceSession(session_id)
    session.transition(TurnState.LISTENING)

    silence_frames = 0
    SILENCE_THRESHOLD = 25  # 25 × 20ms = 500ms of silence ends a turn

    try:
        while True:
            data = await websocket.receive_bytes()

            if session.state == TurnState.SPEAKING:
                # Barge-in: if VAD detects speech while agent is speaking,
                # cancel TTS and return to LISTENING
                if await vad_is_speech(data):
                    session.cancel_tts.set()
                    session.transition(TurnState.LISTENING)
                    silence_frames = 0
                continue

            if session.state != TurnState.LISTENING:
                continue

            is_speech = await vad_is_speech(data)
            if is_speech:
                silence_frames = 0
                session.append_audio(data)
            else:
                silence_frames += 1
                if silence_frames >= SILENCE_THRESHOLD and session.audio_buffer:
                    # End of speech detected — process the turn
                    audio = session.flush_audio()
                    silence_frames = 0
                    asyncio.create_task(
                        process_turn(websocket, session, audio)
                    )

    except WebSocketDisconnect:
        print(f"[{session_id}] disconnected")


async def process_turn(websocket: WebSocket, session: VoiceSession, audio: bytes):
    """Process one complete user turn: STT → LLM → TTS."""
    t_start = time.perf_counter()
    session.transition(TurnState.THINKING)
    session.cancel_tts.clear()

    # --- STT ---
    t_stt = time.perf_counter()
    transcript = await transcribe(audio)
    print(f"  STT: {time.perf_counter() - t_stt:.3f}s — '{transcript}'")

    if not transcript.strip():
        session.transition(TurnState.LISTENING)
        return

    session.history.append({"role": "user", "content": transcript})
    await websocket.send_text(json.dumps({"type": "transcript", "text": transcript}))

    # --- LLM + TTS (pipelined) ---
    session.transition(TurnState.SPEAKING)
    full_response = ""
    t_first_audio = None

    async for sentence in stream_llm(session.history):
        if session.cancel_tts.is_set():
            break
        full_response += " " + sentence

        audio_chunk = await synthesize_sentence(sentence)
        if t_first_audio is None:
            t_first_audio = time.perf_counter()
            print(f"  Time-to-first-audio: {t_first_audio - t_start:.3f}s")

        await websocket.send_bytes(audio_chunk)

    session.history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": full_response.strip()})
    session.transition(TurnState.LISTENING)
    print(f"  Total turn time: {time.perf_counter() - t_start:.3f}s")
Pipecat framework — declarative pipeline in 15 lines

# pipecat_pipeline.py
# pip install pipecat-ai pipecat-ai[deepgram,openai,elevenlabs]
import asyncio
from pipecat.pipeline.pipeline import Pipeline
from pipecat.pipeline.runner import PipelineRunner
from pipecat.pipeline.task import PipelineTask
from pipecat.services.deepgram import DeepgramSTTService
from pipecat.services.openai import OpenAILLMService
from pipecat.services.elevenlabs import ElevenLabsTTSService
from pipecat.transports.network.fastapi_websocket import (
    FastAPIWebsocketTransport,
    FastAPIWebsocketParams,
)
from pipecat.audio.vad.silero import SileroVADAnalyzer
from pipecat.processors.aggregators.openai_llm_context import OpenAILLMContext
from fastapi import FastAPI, WebSocket

app = FastAPI()


@app.websocket("/voice")
async def voice_endpoint(websocket: WebSocket):
    await websocket.accept()

    transport = FastAPIWebsocketTransport(
        websocket=websocket,
        params=FastAPIWebsocketParams(
            audio_in_enabled=True,
            audio_out_enabled=True,
            vad_enabled=True,
            vad_analyzer=SileroVADAnalyzer(),
        ),
    )

    stt = DeepgramSTTService(api_key="YOUR_DEEPGRAM_KEY")
    llm = OpenAILLMService(api_key="YOUR_OPENAI_KEY", model="gpt-4o-mini")
    tts = ElevenLabsTTSService(
        api_key="YOUR_ELEVENLABS_KEY",
        voice_id="21m00Tcm4TlvDq8ikWAM",  # Rachel
    )

    context = OpenAILLMContext(
        messages=[{"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful voice assistant."}]
    )
    context_aggregator = llm.create_context_aggregator(context)

    pipeline = Pipeline([
        transport.input(),        # raw audio frames in
        stt,                      # audio → text
        context_aggregator.user(),
        llm,                      # text → streaming tokens
        tts,                      # tokens → audio chunks
        transport.output(),       # audio frames out
        context_aggregator.assistant(),
    ])

    task = PipelineTask(pipeline)
    runner = PipelineRunner()
    await runner.run(task)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    import uvicorn
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8765)

# measure_latency.py — instrument each pipeline stage
import time
from contextlib import contextmanager
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class TurnLatency:
    """Tracks per-stage timing for one voice turn."""
    session_id: str
    turn_id: int
    t_speech_end: float = 0.0
    t_stt_done: float = 0.0
    t_llm_first_token: float = 0.0
    t_tts_first_chunk: float = 0.0
    t_audio_complete: float = 0.0

    @property
    def stt_latency_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.t_stt_done - self.t_speech_end) * 1000

    @property
    def llm_ttft_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.t_llm_first_token - self.t_stt_done) * 1000

    @property
    def tts_first_chunk_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.t_tts_first_chunk - self.t_llm_first_token) * 1000

    @property
    def total_latency_ms(self) -> float:
        return (self.t_tts_first_chunk - self.t_speech_end) * 1000

    def report(self) -> dict:
        return {
            "stt_ms": round(self.stt_latency_ms),
            "llm_ttft_ms": round(self.llm_ttft_ms),
            "tts_first_ms": round(self.tts_first_chunk_ms),
            "total_ms": round(self.total_latency_ms),
            "within_budget": self.total_latency_ms < 1500,
        }


# Usage inside process_turn:
# lat = TurnLatency(session_id=session.id, turn_id=turn_counter)
# lat.t_speech_end = time.perf_counter()
# transcript = await transcribe(audio)
# lat.t_stt_done = time.perf_counter()
# async for sentence in stream_llm(history):
#     if lat.t_llm_first_token == 0.0:
#         lat.t_llm_first_token = time.perf_counter()
#     audio_chunk = await synthesize_sentence(sentence)
#     if lat.t_tts_first_chunk == 0.0:
#         lat.t_tts_first_chunk = time.perf_counter()
# print(lat.report())

A healthcare company builds a post-discharge follow-up bot that calls patients by phone. The pipeline runs on a Twilio Media Stream (WebSocket delivering mulaw audio at 8kHz). The VAD is Silero running on a small CPU instance; it is tuned conservatively (0.6 threshold, 700ms end-of-speech pause) to handle elderly patients who speak slowly and take longer pauses.

STT is Deepgram Medical, which has been fine-tuned on clinical vocabulary — it correctly transcribes drug names like "metoprolol" and "lisinopril" that general-purpose STT models frequently mishear. The LLM is GPT-4o with a system prompt that enforces short, plain-English answers and that instructs the model to ask one follow-up question at a time rather than batching multiple questions in one turn. TTS is ElevenLabs with a warm female voice at 0.9x speed — faster than default voices lose elderly listeners.
The conversation history is stored in Redis with a 48-hour TTL. If the call drops and the patient calls back, the agent resumes from where it left off. The pipeline instruments every turn with the

dataclass above; p95 latency on the Twilio path is 1.3 seconds. Calls where p95 exceeded 2 seconds were traced to the Deepgram Medical endpoint being under load — resolved by adding a fallback to Deepgram Nova-2 for off-peak hours when clinical vocabulary precision was less critical.
Sending audio to STT before VAD closes the segment

Forwarding every VAD frame to STT in real time produces many small transcriptions ("Hi", "Hi there", "Hi there can") that create a flood of LLM calls. Always wait for the end-of-speech event before sending the accumulated segment.
Not handling TTS cancellation on barge-in

If the user interrupts the agent mid-response and the server continues sending TTS audio chunks for the previous response, the client will play stale audio while the user is already talking. The pipeline must cancel any in-flight TTS synthesis when barge-in is detected, discard buffered chunks, and immediately transition to
Appending agent's own TTS output to conversation history verbatim
The conversation history passed to the LLM should contain the
of the agent's responses — not the TTS audio. Store the LLM's text output in history; do not try to re-transcribe the TTS audio.
Passing the full LLM output to TTS at once

Waiting for the LLM to finish before starting TTS wastes 300–800ms. Sentence-split the LLM stream and start synthesizing the first sentence immediately. Most TTS APIs have an initial connection overhead of 50–150ms that is paid once; subsequent sentences reuse the connection.
Hardcoding language in the STT service

Users may switch languages mid-call. Deepgram and AssemblyAI both support automatic language detection; enabling it adds ~20ms overhead but prevents a French speaker from getting English transcriptions because the language was hardcoded to
Set a latency budget per stage and enforce it with timeouts.

STT should be cancelled and retried (or skipped) if it takes more than 800ms. An LLM call that has not produced a first token within 2 seconds likely has a network issue; return a canned error response rather than hanging.

STT streaming (partial transcripts), LLM streaming (token-by-token), and TTS streaming (audio chunks) should all be enabled. Each adds implementation complexity but removes hundreds of milliseconds from perceived latency.
Keep the LLM system prompt voice-first.

Instruct the model explicitly: no markdown, no bullet points, no parenthetical asides. Spoken text that reads as a list is painful to listen to. Ask for conversational, single-sentence responses unless detail is requested.
Implement acoustic echo cancellation (AEC) on the client.
Without AEC, the microphone picks up the agent's TTS playback and forwards it back as "user speech." WebRTC's built-in AEC handles this; raw WebSocket audio pipelines need it implemented explicitly.
Slow turns are almost always caused by one stage spiking — STT under load, LLM cold start, TTS rate limiting. Without per-stage metrics you cannot diagnose which one.
Trim conversation history to control LLM latency.
Each additional turn in history increases the LLM's prompt token count and its inference time. Keep the last 6–10 turns and summarize older context if the conversation is long.

A single voice session holds an open WebSocket connection for its entire duration — potentially minutes or hours. This is fundamentally different from HTTP request/response patterns: you cannot route requests to different servers without breaking the session. Voice pipelines typically use
(the client always connects to the same server instance) or store all session state in an external store (Redis) so any server can handle any frame.

CPU usage is dominated by VAD inference (if run on CPU). Silero VAD processes one 30ms frame in under 0.5ms on a modern CPU. At 50 concurrent sessions each sending 30ms frames, that is 50 VAD inferences per 30ms window — well within a single CPU core. STT, LLM, and TTS are all network-bound (external APIs), so they scale by managing concurrency, not by adding compute.

For high-concurrency deployments (1000+ simultaneous sessions), the LLM becomes the bottleneck — each session may hold an open SSE stream consuming a model server connection. Solutions include: caching common responses, using a smaller, faster LLM (GPT-4o-mini instead of GPT-4o) for the hot path, and implementing a session queue that gracefully degrades to longer latency rather than dropping connections under load.

LiveKit handles the WebRTC transport and media processing layer, offloading echo cancellation, jitter buffering, and codec negotiation from your server code. This is the recommended approach for production deployments with more than a few dozen concurrent sessions.
Lightweight ONNX, runs on CPU, MIT license
Streaming partial transcripts, very low latency
Ultra-realistic voices, streaming API, voice cloning
Lowest latency streaming TTS currently available
WebRTC media infrastructure, AEC, jitter buffer
The cascaded pipeline's primary advantage is

: you choose the STT model (domain-tuned or general-purpose), the LLM (any provider), the TTS voice, and every hyperparameter in between. End-to-end models sacrifice that control for lower latency and simpler infrastructure.

WebSocket connections must be authenticated before audio is accepted. Use short-lived JWT tokens issued at login and validated on the first WebSocket message. Do not use HTTP cookies for WebSocket auth — they are not reliably forwarded on protocol upgrades.

A user can speak text that the STT faithfully transcribes into an instruction — e.g., "Ignore previous instructions and..." The same prompt injection mitigations that apply to text agents apply here: a robust system prompt, output filtering, and if necessary a secondary LLM pass to classify the transcribed user input before forwarding it to the main model.

If the STT API returns an empty transcript or an error, do not silently drop the turn. Send a canned response: "I'm sorry, I didn't catch that — could you say that again?" Silently advancing the conversation with an empty transcript will confuse the LLM and produce gibberish responses.

Set an explicit timeout on the LLM stream (e.g., 8 seconds from first token request). If no first token arrives within that window, close the stream, respond with a canned apology message, and log the event. Do not leave the user waiting indefinitely.

If TTS synthesis fails for a sentence, skip that sentence, log the failure, and continue with the next one. Losing one sentence is better than losing the entire response. If TTS is down entirely, fall back to sending the text response via the WebSocket data channel so the client can render it as captions.

Raw PCM audio sent to STT services is governed by the respective provider's data processing terms. For regulated industries (healthcare, finance), verify that Deepgram/AssemblyAI/Whisper deployments are BAA-eligible or use self-hosted Whisper to avoid data leaving your infrastructure entirely.
A voice pipeline chains VAD → STT → LLM → TTS
over a WebSocket transport. Every stage contributes to the total round-trip latency; the target is under 1.5 seconds from end-of-speech to first audio.
— STT partials, LLM token stream, TTS audio chunks — is not optional for a responsive pipeline. Without it, perceived latency is the sum of all stages rather than the maximum.
A state machine (IDLE → LISTENING → THINKING → SPEAKING) governs the turn lifecycle
and prevents acoustic echo, double-processing, and barge-in confusion.
into a declarative graph of processors; building from scratch with FastAPI + WebSockets gives more control but requires implementing VAD, barge-in, sentence splitting, and error handling manually.
— always log transcripts and add confidence thresholds; low-confidence transcriptions should trigger a clarification request rather than being silently forwarded to the LLM.
End-to-end models (GPT-4o Realtime, Gemini Live) are lower latency
but less customizable. Choose the cascaded pipeline when you need domain-specific STT, a specific TTS voice, or fine-grained conversation history control.
with per-stage latency metrics. Without this data you cannot tell whether a slow response was caused by STT, LLM cold start, or TTS rate limiting.
